title: Qubit-efficient and gate-efficient encodings of graph partitioning problems for quantum optimization
authors: Tristan Zaborniak, Prashanti Priya Angara, Vikram Khipple Mulligan, Hausi Müller, Ulrike Stege
abstract: We introduce a qubit- and gate-efficient higher-order unconstrained binary optimization (HUBO) encoding for graph partitioning problems requiring label-count minimization. This widely applicable class of problems includes minimum graph coloring, minimum $k$-cut, and community detection. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to address the optimization versions of these problems in a quantum setting, rather than only their decision counterparts. Our construction encodes each $k$-valued vertex variable using $\lceil \log_2 k \rceil$ bits and employs a novel lexicographic penalty system that implicitly minimizes partition count without requiring dedicated indicator variables. We derive provably sufficient conditions on all penalty coefficients, including those arising from Rosenberg quadratization, guaranteeing feasibility and optimality of the lowest-energy solution. Analogous conditions are derived for a one-hot encoding to enable controlled comparison. We also show that our encoding reduces two-qubit gate count per QAOA layer from $Θ(|V||k|^2 + |E||k|)$ for the one-hot encoding to $Θ(|E| \cdot |k| \lceil\log_2 |k|\rceil)$. Benchmarking on a quantum annealer demonstrates that our logarithmic encoding significantly improves solution quality and time-to-solution for minimum graph coloring relative to one-hot encoding, with greater advantage as problem size increases.
summary: This paper presents a new way to map graph partitioning problems onto quantum hardware using significantly fewer qubits and gates. By using a logarithmic encoding instead of the traditional one-hot method, the authors reduce the resource requirements for problems like graph coloring. They introduce a clever penalty system that allows the quantum computer to find the minimum number of colors without extra variables. Experimental tests on a D-Wave quantum annealer show that this method drastically improves the speed and quality of solutions as problem sizes grow.
main_problem: Efficiently encoding graph partitioning problems, such as minimum graph coloring, for quantum optimization to reduce the qubit and gate overhead required on near-term quantum hardware.
main_result: The proposed logarithmic encoding achieves the information-theoretic minimum of qubits per vertex and significantly reduces two-qupi gate counts, leading to a 1-2 order of magnitude improvement in time-to-solution on a quantum annealer.
method: A novel higher-order unconstrained binary optimization (HUBO) encoding using a lexicographic penalty system to minimize partition count, combined with Rosenberg quadratization to reduce terms to QUBO.
model_or_system: The study utilizes the D-Wave Advantage 2_1.11 quantum annealer (superconducting flux qubits on a Zephyr graph) and compares one-hot encoding against a new logarithmic encoding for label-symmetric graph partitioning problems.
key_observables: Time-to-solution (TTS), logical and physical qubit counts, CNOT gate counts, and chain length variance.
important_parameters: Number of vertices (|V|), number of edges (|E|), number of possible labels (k), and penalty coefficients for feasibility and optimality.
main_assumptions: The encoding assumes the problem class is label-symmetric and pairwise-decomposable; the derived penalty bounds are sufficient but potentially conservative.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates encoding differences; Figure 2 shows TTS increasing with graph size, logical qubit counts (with an inset for pre-quadratization), physical qubit counts after embedding, and chain length variance.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem and encoding types, presents the mathematical construction and lexicographic penalty system, provides proofs for penalty coefficient sufficiency, performs complexity and gate count analysis, and concludes with benchmarking on a quantum annealer.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Small example graph colored improperly (upper row, violating edge colored red) and properly (bottom row). The tables contain bitstring assignments to each vertex under the one-hot and logarithmic encodings. Note that with 4 (|V |) vertices, 4 (|V |) qubits are required per vertex for one-hot encoding, and 2 (⌈log2 |V |⌉) qubits are required for logarithmic encoding. While the bitstrings are colored for visualization purposes, they do not refer to a particular color.

Fig. 2: Upper left: TTS as a function of |V |. Upper right: Post- quadratization logical qubits per encoding; inset: pre-quadratization. Lower left: Physical qubits after minor embedding. Lower right: Chain length variance; inset: average chain length.
